seed: safety vs exposure; pressure_vs_release; split_vertical; echo_right; braid against ripple; pressure_border; thin_vs_heavy; vessel; softness_severity | abstract variation request 430; target batch count 500
title: Exposure vs Shelter
reasoning: This composition embodies the emotional tension between the concepts of safety and exposure. The palette features a blend of warm earthy colors and cool delicate shades, providing a contrast that captures both the weight of safety (warm brown and shelter green) and the lightness of exposure (pale skin and cold white). The left side showcases a 'flame' pattern representing safety through warmth, characterized by deep, enveloping colors. Conversely, the right features a 'flood' pattern symbolizing exposure, characterized by lighter, more open colors that invite a sense of air and vulnerability. The main collision occurs at the center, where the warm brown meets pale skin in a transition zone, emphasizing the struggle between protective enclosure and unguarded exposure. This creates a dynamic flow, particularly in the diagonal tension where different emotional states converge, making the overall design a narrative of balance and imbalance.

layout.name: split_vertical
layout.description: Left 4 columns are emotion A, right 4 columns are emotion B.

composition.name: exposure_vs_shelter
composition.idea: A pale exposed center is either protected by a frame or left open to incoming marks.

referenceExample.title: Repair After Guilt
referenceExample.pattern: dark residue patched by small ivory and gold marks

generationRules: Pick 1 pattern type for emotion A.
Pick 1 pattern type for emotion B.
Pick 1 shape seed as the central object.
Apply 1 to 6 broken pixels to create imperfection.
Leave at least 10 percent negative space unless the mood is panic, rage, urgency, euphoria, or pressure.
Use the shape seed as an abstract organizing metaphor, not as a literal icon.
Make the two selected pattern types visibly collide, overlap, interrupt, or trade space.
Prefer 4 to 8 distinct hex colors, with at least one quiet field color and one accent color.
Keep the 8x8 grid readable at thumbnail size by preserving clear large-scale structure.
Let one emotion dominate 55 to 70 percent of the square unless the selected layout demands balance.
Use broken pixels as intentional disruptions, scars, repairs, leaks, or false echoes.
Avoid perfectly even distribution unless the chosen composition is control, serenity, clarity, or certainty.
Give the main collision a location: center, edge, corner, diagonal, border, or channel.
generationSpec.negativeSpaceRule: Leave at least 10 percent negative space unless the chosen emotional tone needs dense pressure.
